definition: Hierarchical clustering builds a nested tree of clusters (a dendrogram) by repeatedly merging nearby groups (agglomerative) or splitting groups apart (divisive), using pairwise distances and a linkage rule to decide which clusters combine next.
layman: Instead of picking k upfront, hierarchical clustering shows how points gradually group together like a family tree—you can later cut the tree at different heights to get coarse or fine clusters.
content:
## Two strategies: agglomerative and divisive

**Hierarchical clustering** organizes data into a hierarchy of nested clusters. The result is usually drawn as a **dendrogram**: a tree whose leaves are individual points and whose internal nodes represent merges (or splits).

### Agglomerative (bottom-up) — the common default

1. Start with each of the n points as its own cluster.
2. Compute distances between all pairs of clusters using a **linkage** rule.
3. Merge the two closest clusters.
4. Repeat until one cluster contains all points.

Each merge step adds a level to the dendrogram. The **height** at which two branches join records the dissimilarity at which those groups were combined.

### Divisive (top-down) — less common in practice

1. Start with all points in one cluster.
2. Split the cluster into two sub-clusters that are maximally separated.
3. Recursively split until each point is alone.

Divisive methods can be more expensive to implement well; most libraries default to agglomerative clustering.

## Linkage methods: how cluster distance is defined

Given clusters A and B, linkage defines **d(A, B)** from pairwise point distances:

| Linkage | Cluster distance rule | Typical effect |
| --- | --- | --- |
| **Single** | Minimum distance between any point in A and any point in B | Tends to follow chains; can produce elongated clusters |
| **Complete** | Maximum distance between any pair across clusters | Favors compact, similarly sized clusters |
| **Average** | Mean distance over all cross pairs | A compromise between single and complete |
| **Ward** | Increase in total within-cluster variance if A and B merge | Often works well with Euclidean distance on standardized features |

Linkage choice changes the shape of clusters even when the underlying point distances are identical.

## Reading a dendrogram and choosing k

- **Vertical axis** (or branch height): dissimilarity at merge time—higher joins mean more different groups were combined.
- **Horizontal cuts**: draw a line at a chosen height; the number of vertical lines it crosses gives the number of clusters at that resolution.
- **No single correct k**: the tree lets you explore multiple granularities without re-running the algorithm from scratch (unlike k-means, which fixes k before fitting).

## Distance metrics and scaling

Hierarchical clustering depends entirely on how you measure distance between points (Euclidean, Manhattan, cosine, etc.) and whether features are on comparable scales. Distance definitions and normalization are covered in **Norms and Distances**; here we assume a chosen metric and, when needed, standardized features.

## Complexity and when to use it

Agglomerative clustering with naive implementations is often **O(n³)** time and **O(n²)** memory for storing the distance matrix—fine for hundreds to a few thousand points, painful for millions. For large n, prefer k-means, mini-batch methods, or DBSCAN, and use hierarchical clustering on samples or prototypes.

## Relation to other clustering methods

- **k-means** partitions into exactly k groups by optimizing centroids; it does not produce a merge tree.
- **Cluster evaluation** metrics (silhouette, Davies–Bouldin, etc.) help compare cuts of a dendrogram or validate cluster quality.
- Hierarchical clustering is a core **unsupervised learning** technique when exploratory structure and multi-scale groupings matter more than raw speed.
keyPoints: Agglomerative clustering merges closest clusters bottom-up; divisive clustering splits top-down.
A dendrogram records merge history; cutting at different heights yields different numbers of clusters.
Linkage (single, complete, average, Ward) defines inter-cluster distance and strongly affects cluster shape.
Point distances and feature scaling come from your chosen metric—see Norms and Distances.
Naive agglomerative methods scale poorly to very large n; use for moderate-sized exploratory analysis.
